title: The Rollout

jokes.text: What possible difference could one week make? / We made sure to order those products in time for the rollout.
jokes.setup: David dismissively asks what difference a week could make with the delivery
jokes.punchline: Stevie reveals the entire carefully planned rollout was built around that exact timing, making his casualness absurd
jokes.explanation: Classic David obliviousness — he dismisses the exact problem his negligence caused. The irony is character-specific and lands well as an opener establishing the episode's running gag.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I may have taught you that word incorrectly.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: What do you mean?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yeah, I've known for a while, but I thought it was funny.

jokes.text: Um, who taught you rollout? May have been me. Sorry.
jokes.setup: Stevie keeps enthusiastically using 'rollout' as business jargon, and David is annoyed by it
jokes.punchline: Johnny casually admits he taught her the word, immediately undercutting David's irritation
jokes.explanation: Perfectly economical. The casual 'May have been me. Sorry.' is deadpan gold — Johnny takes responsibility without drama, and the tiny 'Sorry' is perfectly placed. Feels completely in character for both the proud-of-himself Johnny and the exasperated David.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Dad, please stop saying 'rollout.'

jokes.text: Okay, you really need to stop saying rollout.
jokes.setup: Johnny and Stevie keep enthusiastically deploying the word 'rollout' with pride
jokes.punchline: David's exasperation with the word itself — the joke is that the business term has become the source of irritation, not the business problem
jokes.explanation: Functional escalation of the rollout gag. David's frustration with the word is a good character beat but not surprising at this point.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: What did I just say about the rollout?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Dad, stop saying rollout.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, we need to discuss the rollout.

jokes.text: So, I may be looking elsewhere when it comes time for the pop-out store.
jokes.setup: Johnny is threatening David with business consequences, trying to sound authoritative
jokes.punchline: He says 'pop-out store' instead of 'pop-up store,' completely deflating his own power move with the wrong word
jokes.explanation: Excellent character joke — Johnny tries to assert authority with business jargon he doesn't quite have mastered, and the malapropism perfectly punctures the moment. 'Pop-out store' is a great wrong word. Sets up the follow-up beat.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: We need to think outside the box here. I'm talking about a pop-out store.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: A pop-out store?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Yes, you know, where the store just... pops out.

jokes.text: Okay. He meant pop-up store, right? / Honestly, David, it took him like 2 weeks to learn rollout, so...
jokes.setup: Johnny's malapropism 'pop-out store' hangs in the air
jokes.punchline: Stevie's deadpan clarification that it took Johnny two weeks to learn 'rollout' — implying this is just his natural vocabulary level — is the perfect punctuation
jokes.explanation: Great callback that ties together both the rollout gag and the pop-out malapropism. Stevie's delivery (implied dry) makes this the payoff of the scene's running thread. The 'so...' trailing off is perfectly constructed — the audience fills in the rest.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: He doesn't know what a pop-up store is. He thinks it's a store that pops out of the ground.

jokes.text: It's just that we don't want people adopting sad dogs... we want the puppies to be happy, and fun, and flirty.
jokes.setup: Alexis is giving notes on the puppy adoption event
jokes.punchline: She describes the rescue puppies' emotional state as a branding requirement, culminating in 'flirty' — an adjective that cannot reasonably apply to puppies
jokes.explanation: Very Alexis — she treats a charitable animal adoption like a brand activation. 'Flirty' is the key word, completely inappropriate for puppies but perfectly appropriate to Alexis's worldview. The coordinator's deadpan response ('it's kinda hard to guarantee fun') is the straight-man punctuation.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Most of the dogs at the shelter are, um, rescue dogs. So they've been through some trauma. They're not really in a 'flirty' phase right now.

jokes.text: it's kinda hard to guarantee fun.
jokes.setup: Alexis has demanded that rescue dogs be happy, fun, and flirty
jokes.punchline: Ted's quiet, reasonable pushback — 'it's kinda hard to guarantee fun' for traumatized rescue animals — is played completely straight
jokes.explanation: The comedy is in the understated reasonableness against Alexis's absurdity. Ted as straight man works well. Slightly undercut by being a reactive beat rather than an originating one.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my god, she's so cute!

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Actually, he's a he. And he's married.

jokes.text: Hmm. Wow, love that for her.
jokes.setup: Ted reveals he's already promised one of the puppies to his friend Heather even though the event is a week away
jokes.punchline: Alexis's 'Wow, love that for her' — a passive-aggressive social media-style platitude that barely conceals her irritation
jokes.explanation: Highly quotable Alexis-ism. The phrase 'love that for her' is perfectly of her character — a social performance that masks jealousy. Economy is exceptional: three words do all the work. This has become something of a cultural phrase in broader use.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, that's so great! I'm really happy for Heather. She's going to be such a good dog mom. Like, honestly, I think she's probably better suited for this than I am anyway. I mean, I'm just so busy all the time, and she's just got so much more love to give, you know? So yeah, no, this is perfect. I'm genuinely thrilled for her.

jokes.text: You know you two are lovely little freaks.
jokes.setup: Moira is observing Alexis and Ted meeting in person rather than digitally
jokes.punchline: She calls them 'lovely little freaks' as an apparent compliment — Moira's inverted social register makes an insult into affection
jokes.explanation: Perfectly economic Moira line — 'lovely little freaks' is simultaneously condescending and warm, which is her entire character in three words. High character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'm hosting a soirée for the rescue puppy initiative. It's a very exclusive gathering of the town's most discerning pet enthusiasts.

jokes.text: Oh, Theodore, back for another meeting of the animals, I see.
jokes.setup: Ted has appeared at another meeting about the puppy adoption event
jokes.punchline: Moira calling it 'a meeting of the animals' — subtly demeaning to both the event and Ted — with faux-gracious greeting using his full name 'Theodore'
jokes.explanation: Moira's aristocratic condescension is well-deployed here. 'Theodore' vs 'Ted' signals her polite dismissal. 'Meeting of the animals' is a nice turn of phrase.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, they made eye contact. That's either the beginning of a beautiful romance or the prelude to a violent crime. There's really no in-between.

jokes.text: In this digital day of discontented disconnection, you two still manage to do things face-to-face, like people from before your time.
jokes.setup: Moira walks in to find Alexis and Ted in a meeting and comments on them
jokes.punchline: Her grandiloquent, overcomplicated way of saying 'you met in person' — complete with alliterative phrase 'discontented disconnection' — is the joke
jokes.explanation: Pure Moira vocabulary joke. The alliteration is pleasingly overdone. The observation itself isn't particularly sharp but the delivery mode is extremely character-specific. Economy suffers because it takes too long to get to the point.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: People will experience a profound sense of joy and fulfillment in their domestic arrangements.

jokes.text: Safe to say our animal-loving singles will be well placated.
jokes.setup: Moira observes the ongoing puppy-adoption planning
jokes.punchline: Using 'placated' — a word implying management of difficult or angry people — for singles who like animals. The misapplication of the word, or her dismissive view of single people, is the joke.
jokes.explanation: Moira vocabulary joke — 'placated' is slightly wrong-register for describing happy event attendees, suggesting she views the singles as a crowd to be managed rather than people to be served. Functional character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: The venue called to cancel. Apparently, there was a scheduling conflict with their annual Squirt and Twirl.

jokes.text: That nudnick at the curling rink just informed me that they'll no longer be able to accommodate our Lover's Messy Sloppy Joe Eat-a-Thon.
jokes.setup: Moira arrives with a crisis for Alexis to solve
jokes.punchline: The event name 'Lover's Messy Sloppy Joe Eat-a-Thon' — an absurdly specific and deeply unsexy name for a Singles Week event — combined with Moira calling someone a 'nudnick'
jokes.explanation: The event name is the real joke — it's a perfect absurdist construction for a Singles Week event. 'Lover's Messy Sloppy Joe Eat-a-Thon' is simultaneously romantic-aspirational and grotesque. 'Nudnick' is a great Moira word choice. High originality for the name itself.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Alas, I cannot. I've been summoned for jury duty.

jokes.text: Alas! Your co-chair will be busy getting herself out of jury duty today.
jokes.setup: Alexis asks why Moira can't find the new venue herself
jokes.punchline: Moira's theatrical 'alas!' followed by the revelation that she's trying to get OUT of jury duty — framed as a noble obligation — is the comic inversion
jokes.explanation: The theatrical 'alas!' is very Moira. The joke is in framing jury-duty evasion as a noble burden requiring her full attention. Functional character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I would never overstep. I respect boundaries.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: That said, Alexis, your presentation was rather flat, and Ted, your tie choice was actively working against you.

jokes.text: Wow! Looking very nice, Stevie. / Yeah, thanks, except I think I have to go home now.
jokes.setup: Johnny compliments Stevie on looking nice
jokes.punchline: Stevie immediately says she has to go home — the compliment was premature and something is clearly very wrong
jokes.explanation: Nice misdirection — the compliment sets up normalcy, the immediate 'I have to go home now' tells us something is wrong before we see what. Good economy and structural timing.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: It's not a canine auction, Mother. It's a dog show. And we're not spending the whole day there.

jokes.text: Nothing, just rolling things out, like you asked me to.
jokes.setup: Johnny asks what Stevie has been doing differently that might have caused the rash
jokes.punchline: Stevie's 'just rolling things out' — playing on the rollout phrase — lands as both a literal answer and a callback to the running gag
jokes.explanation: Clever callback that earns its place — 'rolling things out' works both as an explanation (using the products) and as a wink to the episode's running gag. The dual meaning is efficiently deployed.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Is that my lotion?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: No.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You're using my lotion.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I have a rash.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That's a guest lotion. You can't use guest lotion on a rash.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Well, it's too late now.

jokes.text: Uh I know it's for the guests, but it's like really good stuff, and this is extra.
jokes.setup: Johnny catches Stevie still applying the lotion to her rashy arms even while they're trying to figure out if it's the cause
jokes.punchline: Stevie's justification — 'I know it's for the guests, but it's extra' — reveals she's been using motel products on herself and can't stop despite the possible allergic reaction
jokes.explanation: Good Stevie characterization — her pragmatic, somewhat chaotic self-care is funny in context. The 'it's extra' justification is very her.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: What have you been doing differently?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I've just been rolling out new ideas for the business.

jokes.text: Before or after your arm started itching? / After, definitely after. [beat] I might've tried a little bit when I was unpacking the box, but... my skin gets real dry in here.
jokes.setup: Johnny asks if Stevie started using the lotion before or after her arm itched — a simple question with an obvious 'correct' answer
jokes.punchline: Stevie insists 'after, definitely after' then immediately admits she tried it when unpacking — so definitely before — with the excuse that her skin is dry
jokes.explanation: Strong structure — the confident 'definitely after' followed by the immediate self-contradiction is a classic comedic reversal, but it's executed with perfect Stevie energy. The 'my skin gets real dry in here' excuse is the cherry on top. Good escalation within a tight exchange.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Because my skin is dry. But also because I don't want to use too much of it, so I'm rationing it. But then my skin gets drier, so I use more. It's a vicious cycle, David, and frankly, I don't appreciate the interrogation.

jokes.text: I don't know, okay! It might be causing the rash, but it's also the only thing that's making it feel better.
jokes.setup: Stevie is caught continuing to apply the lotion that may be causing her rash
jokes.punchline: Her completely logical-yet-absurd explanation — the thing potentially causing the problem is also the only thing providing relief — stated with total Stevie pragmatism
jokes.explanation: Genuinely funny absurdist logic delivered straight. It's also thematically resonant (the thing causing you harm that you can't stop) but the surface comedy is the baffling self-awareness paired with continued self-infliction.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: We need to do a rollout of the new product line.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Johnny, didn't we just discuss this? The rash—

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: A full rollout. Nationwide.

jokes.text: This is the last thing we need in the middle of a rollout!
jokes.setup: Crisis of the rash has emerged; they're rushing to deal with it
jokes.punchline: Johnny drops 'rollout' again — he's thoroughly absorbed the word David hates — as a callback capstone for the opening scene's gag
jokes.explanation: Functional running gag beat — it does the job of keeping the word alive through the episode. Not the freshest hit of the gag but serves its purpose.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Hi, I'm Alexis Rose, of course.

jokes.text: And oh, for those of you asking about the t-shirts, yes, they are supposed to be tight. This is Singles Week, everyone is fair game.
jokes.setup: Alexis is addressing the volunteer group
jokes.punchline: The parenthetical about tight t-shirts — 'everyone is fair game' — reveals she's designed the volunteers as marketing objects for the dating event, not functional workers
jokes.explanation: Good Alexis joke — treating her volunteers as Singles Week participants/attractions rather than helpers is perfectly on-brand. 'Everyone is fair game' is a nice predatory undercut.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Why are you back? I thought you were going to be gone for another week.

dialogue.speaker: Mutt
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, my dad's having a baby, so I figured I'd come back.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: What?

dialogue.speaker: Mutt
dialogue.line: My stepmom is pregnant.

jokes.text: I asked a friend of mine to keep an eye on it while I was away, and musta slipped her mind. / It didn't slip my mind, I just like, didn't go.
jokes.setup: Mutt explains that the barn got messy because he asked a friend to watch it
jokes.punchline: Alexis is standing right there and reveals she was the friend — and didn't even pretend she forgot, she just didn't bother
jokes.explanation: Excellent structural joke — Mutt politely covers with 'musta slipped her mind' (offering Alexis an excuse), and she immediately abandons it with complete unabashed honesty: 'I just like, didn't go.' The absence of guilt or excuse is the joke. Very high character integration — this is quintessential Alexis. Economy is excellent.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, I completely forgot about the barn. I didn't even know we had a barn.

jokes.text: Yeah, a nice little home for a family of raccoons. I had to evict them, though, and they didn't go quietly.
jokes.setup: Alexis asks if the barn is bad
jokes.punchline: Mutt's description of raccoon eviction with the deadpan 'they didn't go quietly' — framing an animal encounter with the language of a landlord-tenant dispute
jokes.explanation: Nice deadpan — treating raccoon eviction like a difficult tenant situation is a pleasingly absurd frame. 'They didn't go quietly' is the well-chosen phrase. Good economy.

dialogue.speaker: Mutt
dialogue.line: Yeah, so the raccoons have been here for about three years now. I've tried everything to get them out, but they've basically established squatter's rights at this point. I even left them an eviction notice, but they can't read, so that didn't really work.

jokes.text: Mutt, you're making me feel partially responsible.
jokes.setup: Mutt describes all the damage (raccoons, dead plants) that resulted from Alexis not checking on the barn
jokes.punchline: 'Partially' responsible — when she is entirely responsible, and her casualness about it is the joke
jokes.explanation: 'Partially' is the key word — Alexis's fractional acknowledgment of total responsibility is a great character beat. Quick and economical.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I can help you water the plants.

dialogue.speaker: Mutt
dialogue.line: They're all dead.

jokes.text: You've served so many times already! I mean you had to miss the Jazzagals Luau Luncheon because of that hung jury, and then before that, you and Johnny got caught in that criminal trial and couldn't make it to the Seafood Potluck.
jokes.setup: Jocelyn is surprised to see Moira at jury duty
jokes.punchline: Jocelyn measures the burden of jury duty entirely by which social events were missed — 'hung jury' leads to missing a Luau Luncheon, 'criminal trial' leads to missing a Seafood Potluck
jokes.explanation: Excellent Jocelyn character joke — her small-town social calendar is the lens through which all of life's events are measured, including the justice system. The specificity of 'Jazzagals Luau Luncheon' and 'Seafood Potluck' make this work.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I may have let a few things slide.

jokes.text: I feel like I'm on 'The Voice'!
jokes.setup: Jocelyn excitedly describes being called by number to stand before a judge in jury selection
jokes.punchline: Her comparison of jury duty to 'The Voice' (a singing competition) — capturing her small-town excitement at any formal process — is the joke
jokes.explanation: Good character beat for Jocelyn — her genuine excitement at civic participation filtered through reality TV is very on-brand. A bit expected as a type of joke but executed cleanly.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Jury duty sounds absolutely exhausting. You have my deepest sympathies.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Oh, it's going to be amazing! I might get sequestered! Do you know what that means? It means I get to stay in a hotel for FREE!

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: And I get to decide someone's fate. It's like being on a game show, except instead of winning a toaster, I'm determining guilt or innocence!

jokes.text: Ooh, as seductive as that sounds, no thank you. With Singles Week just around the corner, my services are desperately needed elsewhere.
jokes.setup: Jocelyn is excited about standing before the judge; Moira is trying to get out of jury duty
jokes.punchline: 'As seductive as that sounds' — Moira's theatrical dismissal treats jury duty as an alluring temptation she must nobly resist
jokes.explanation: Moira's ironic framing of jury duty as seductive is pleasingly theatrical. Functional character comedy, nothing exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'm going to tell them I'm clairvoyant. That way, they'll have to dismiss me because I'll know what the verdict is before the trial even begins.

jokes.text: Jocelyn, you're about to witness a master class in judicatory persuasion.
jokes.setup: Moira announces she's going to get herself off jury duty with her dramatic skills
jokes.punchline: The pompous setup — 'master class in judicatory persuasion' — sets up her eventual spectacular failure in court
jokes.explanation: Strong dramatic irony setup — 'judicatory persuasion' is a great Moira phrase, and the confidence sets up the expected failure. 'Judicatory' as a word choice is perfectly Moira. Rewatch bonus because you know what's coming.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: David, your security is absolutely abysmal. I walked right in here without anyone stopping me. There could be anyone wandering around this store.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Dad, you own the store.

jokes.text: No one at the counter! Who runs a business like this? Anyone could just walk in here and shoplift.
jokes.setup: Moira enters Rose Apothecary to find no one at the counter
jokes.punchline: Her complaint is framed as a business concern, but the specific example she gives — 'anyone could shoplift' — is more like practical advice for criminals than concern for David's business
jokes.explanation: Decent Moira entrance joke — the shoplifting observation is slightly unexpected but not particularly original. Functional.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I look like Phantom of the Opera if he gave up and let the mask win.

jokes.text: David! Your face! / I know my face! I know my face! / It's a heat rash or something! I look like the Phantom of the Opera!
jokes.setup: Moira walks in to find David behind the counter
jokes.punchline: David's face is covered in a rash; his panicked 'I know my face! I know my face!' and his own self-diagnosis as 'the Phantom of the Opera' is the comedic payoff
jokes.explanation: 'I know my face! I know my face!' is a great panicked line — the repetition signals maximum distress. 'Phantom of the Opera' as self-diagnosis is very David: dramatic, theatrical, and oddly specific. High character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: You might be contagious.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm not contagious, Dad.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, you've been looking pretty rough lately. When's the last time you got laid?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm not discussing my sex life with you.

jokes.text: Patrick's at some business seminar, I don't really know, because I wasn't really listening.
jokes.setup: Moira asks why David is alone at the counter
jokes.punchline: David casually admits he doesn't know where his business partner is because he wasn't paying attention when Patrick told him — total Davidian self-absorption
jokes.explanation: Classic David self-revelation — admitting he wasn't listening with no guilt or attempt to cover it up. 'I don't really know, because I wasn't really listening' is very quotable as a David-ism.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: David, your skincare line is causing people to break out in a rash. We need to halt the rollout immediately.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Dad, it's not the products. The customers aren't following the instructions.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, the only thing rolling out right now is hives on people's faces. Cancel the rollout.

jokes.text: Stevie, show him your arms. / Thought you'd never ask!
jokes.setup: Moira instructs Stevie to show David her rashy arms as evidence
jokes.punchline: Stevie's delighted 'Thought you'd never ask!' — delivered as if showing off something good rather than a gross rash
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy — 'Thought you'd never ask!' applied to showing off a rash is a great inversion of the phrase. Stevie's dry enthusiasm makes this land. One of the cleaner joke structures in the episode.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: We need to recall these products immediately. Roll it back.

jokes.text: Your dad thinks it's your moisturizer. / Okay, that's impossible. I've been using this moisturizer every day for 3 months. [beat] Although, this is a new batch.
jokes.setup: David confidently rejects the idea that his moisturizer is causing the rash
jokes.punchline: His own immediate walk-back — 'although, this is a new batch' — and then the dawning horror as he realizes he's sold half the contaminated stock
jokes.explanation: Excellent structure — the confident denial followed by an immediate self-undermining detail is a perfectly constructed reversal. 'Although, this is a new batch' lands as a beat of dawning horror that escalates. The escalation to 'we've already sold half the stock' is well-earned. High structure and character integration scores.

dialogue.speaker: Mutt
dialogue.line: Yeah, it didn't work out. She wanted different things.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: That's so sad. But you know what? That just means she's not your soulmate. Your soulmate is still out there waiting for you.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: And honestly? That's actually really romantic.

jokes.text: See? I knew it! / David, we have a problem. We have a big problem. You could be sued! For retail negligence. / Stevie, show him your arms again. / I don't want to show him my arms. / I don't wanna see her arms again.
jokes.setup: The crisis about the contaminated moisturizer is escalating
jokes.punchline: The rapid-fire three-way exchange — Moira escalating to lawsuits, followed by both Stevie and David simultaneously not wanting to repeat the arm-showing
jokes.explanation: The comedy escalates nicely through the three-way reaction. Moira going straight to 'retail negligence' is excellent character work (theatrical disaster escalation). The simultaneous 'I don't want to'/'I don't wanna' is a good payoff to the arms gag.

dialogue.speaker: Mutt
dialogue.line: So my travel companion was this guy named Moonshine, and we were going to start a cone farm together.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: A cone farm?

dialogue.speaker: Mutt
dialogue.line: Yeah, you know, growing pine cones and selling them to craft stores. It seemed like a really solid business plan at the time.

jokes.text: You realize you're gonna have to talk to Patrick and tell him that you two are gonna have to take a bit of a breather when it comes to... / Ew!
jokes.setup: Moira starts suggesting David needs to warn Patrick about possible contagion
jokes.punchline: She trails off suggestively about their intimate life; David's disgusted 'Ew!' at his mother implying anything about his relationship
jokes.explanation: Classic parent-commenting-on-child's-sex-life cringe. Moira's oblivious intrusion into the topic and David's visceral 'Ew!' are well-matched. The trail-off construction ('when it comes to...') is a good structure.

dialogue.speaker: Mutt
dialogue.line: Yeah, so we got a bit lost on the way back. GPS wasn't working, so we just used the stars to navigate home.

jokes.text: Yeah, and here you were this morning, acting like you were doing us a favour with the rollout.
jokes.setup: David is in crisis over the contaminated products he supplied to the motel
jokes.punchline: Moira triumphantly calls back to his earlier arrogance about the rollout, now that his products are the problem
jokes.explanation: Good callback that serves the character dynamics — Moira gets to be right at David's expense. The word 'rollout' reappearing keeps the gag alive.

dialogue.speaker: Mutt
dialogue.line: Yeah, so we'd pick cones all day, and at the end of the week, if you picked the most cones, you'd get a coupon for a free soft-serve ice cream.

dialogue.speaker: Mutt
dialogue.line: But here's the thing - they rationed how often you could shower. So if you won the ice cream, you had to choose between eating it and using your weekly shower allotment to wash off the cone sap.

dialogue.speaker: Mutt
dialogue.line: Most guys just chose to stay sticky.

jokes.text: Well, we're gonna have to roll it back in.
jokes.setup: They realize all the contaminated products need to be recalled from the motel
jokes.punchline: 'Roll it back in' — a deliberate wordplay on 'rollout' applied to recall, and the pun lands because of how much the rollout gag has been built up
jokes.explanation: This is the best hit of the rollout gag — 'roll it back in' as the perfect wordplay inversion of 'rollout' for a product recall. Economy is exceptional: four words do all the work of the callback. It's earned by everything that came before it. Moira delivering this line is perfect character integration — she's the one who gets to be gleefully right.

dialogue.speaker: Mutt
dialogue.line: Can I get you something to drink? We have water, coffee, or cone wine.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Cone wine?

dialogue.speaker: Mutt
dialogue.line: It's wine served in a cone. Like an ice cream cone, but wine.

jokes.text: And stop touching your face! / You stop touching your face!
jokes.setup: Both Moira and David have rashes and keep touching their faces despite knowing they shouldn't
jokes.punchline: They both catch each other touching their faces and issue simultaneous/immediate scolding, revealing neither can follow their own advice
jokes.explanation: Good physical comedy beat that works entirely through implication (we hear them yelling 'stop touching your face' which tells us both are doing it). The symmetrical exchange — each catching the other doing the same thing — is well-structured.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Creating more work by documenting the work of documenting work

jokes.text: What's happened since you and Tallahassee left on your pine-cone journey?
jokes.setup: Alexis asks Mutt about his ex-girlfriend
jokes.punchline: 'Tallahassee' (wrong name for 'Tennessee') and 'pine-cone journey' (her characterization of a serious life decision) — both in one sentence
jokes.explanation: Double joke in one sentence — wrong name (Alexis can't be bothered to remember) plus the dismissive 'pine-cone journey' reframe. Very Alexis. The fact that she packages a serious romantic quest as a silly errand is the core character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, I hate to break it to you, but that rash ain't from no moisturizer. That's from the poison ivy I've been using to edge the garden beds.

jokes.text: It's uh, Tennessee, and I'm pretty sure you know that.
jokes.setup: Alexis calls his ex 'Tallahassee'
jokes.punchline: Mutt's patient correction — 'I'm pretty sure you know that' — implies Alexis knows perfectly well and just doesn't care enough to get it right
jokes.explanation: 'I'm pretty sure you know that' is a nice understated read on Alexis — he's onto her deliberate carelessness. Good economy and straight-man delivery implied.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, weeds are free, so there's your economical option right there.

jokes.text: Compass broke halfway there, we ended up following a star for 2 days. [...] That day I picked 700 cones! [...] They gave me the Cone of Achievement, which allowed me to take 2 showers that week.
jokes.setup: Mutt describes his pine-cone journey to Tennessee in earnest
jokes.punchline: The entire account is absurdist — following a star for navigation, the 'Cone of Achievement,' the award allowing two showers — delivered with complete sincerity
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's strongest jokes — an absurdist world built with internal logic. 'The Cone of Achievement, which allowed me to take 2 showers that week' is the peak: the award is prestigious in its context, but the tangible perk is hygiene access. Mutt's complete sincerity is what makes it land. High originality and character integration — only Mutt could deliver this story with straight-faced pride.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I have had experiences where I've been completely infatuated with someone, only to discover that they've been taking me for granted. I'm thinking of Brad Pitt circa 1995.

jokes.text: Speaking of showers — Ugh! No!
jokes.setup: Mutt's mention of showering privileges on the cone farm leads him toward a new conversational direction
jokes.punchline: Alexis's preemptive 'Ugh! No!' — she knows exactly where the conversation is going (toward her staying to help) and rejects it instantly
jokes.explanation: Good quick reaction beat — Alexis's preemptive resistance is very her. 'Speaking of showers—' is a nice setup that she immediately shoots down. Economy is strong.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'm trying to be very fair about this embezzlement case. I have no preconceived notions, and I'm certainly not biased against the defendant.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Although, I must say, he does strike me as a prick of a prestidigitator.

jokes.text: I can crack open a bottle of cone wine? Feeling risky?
jokes.setup: Mutt is trying to entice Alexis to stay and help clean the barn
jokes.punchline: 'Cone wine' — wine made from pine cones — is offered as a special treat, delivered as if it's an appealing luxury
jokes.explanation: 'Cone wine' is a wonderful absurdist detail — a natural extension of the cone-farm world, offered with genuine pride. 'Feeling risky?' adds to the joke by framing pine-cone wine as a daring indulgence. Good economy and world-building.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, that was humiliating. I've never been dismissed from a jury before.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Oh, I have. Multiple times. I thought I was doing so well this time. I really had a good feeling about it.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: What happened?

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: The judge asked me if I could be impartial, and I said yes, but apparently my face said no. This was going to be my personal best.

jokes.text: Ugh, fine. Um, no. Uh... fine. No. Okay, but um, like just one.
jokes.setup: Mutt offers cone wine as enticement to stay
jokes.punchline: Alexis's completely conflicted four-position negotiation with herself — 'Ugh, fine. Um, no. Uh... fine. No. Okay, but like just one.' — perfectly captures her inability to commit and her intrinsic desire to stay
jokes.explanation: Alexis's back-and-forth self-negotiation is a great character performance — five position changes in one breath, ending on 'just one' (which we know means more). The rhythm of the line is the joke, and it's very Alexis. High character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Well, I guess that's it for me. I'm off the jury. Finally! Do you know how long I've been waiting for this moment? This is the best day of my life!

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: I mean, who needs to serve on a jury when you can just go home and watch TV? Thank God they realized I'm too fabulous for this courtroom.

jokes.text: this timesheet you have is really complicated. By the time I finished filling it out, I was already into overtime. So do you guys have an overtime sheet I can fill out?
jokes.setup: Roland is clocking out and mentions the timesheet
jokes.punchline: His revelation that filling out the timesheet took so long it created overtime — a self-referential bureaucratic trap — and then asking if there's an overtime sheet for that
jokes.explanation: Great Roland joke — a self-creating bureaucratic absurdity that only Roland would experience without recognizing the irony. Requesting an overtime form to document the overtime caused by the timesheet is a perfect circular logic gag. High character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Mutt
dialogue.line: Well, I mean, we're organizing Singles Week and we're both still single, so I guess we're just two single people throwing a party for single people.

jokes.text: Whoa. Stevie! Geez! Yowzer!
jokes.setup: Roland sees Stevie's rashy arms for the first time
jokes.punchline: Roland's three-part escalating exclamation — 'Whoa. Stevie! Geez! Yowzer!' — is funnier than any single reaction word, and 'Yowzer' as the peak is very Roland
jokes.explanation: 'Yowzer' is the right word for Roland — it's one degree more folksy than expected. The escalating three-part reaction (Whoa / Geez / Yowzer) follows a good comedic rhythm. Another hit of the arms visual gag.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I'm going to go... somewhere else now.

jokes.text: Well, boy, leave it to Stevie to get a rash from a moisturizer when she's sitting right next to poison oak.
jokes.setup: Roland observes Stevie's rash and their conclusion that it's from David's moisturizer
jokes.punchline: Roland — while mocking Stevie — casually reveals the real cause: there's poison oak in a vase right next to her, which is actually Johnny's fault
jokes.explanation: This is structurally excellent — Roland delivers the episode's key revelation (it's poison oak, not the moisturizer) as a casual aside while insulting Stevie. The dramatic irony is perfect: Roland thinks he's pointing out Stevie's bad luck, but the poison oak is in a vase because Johnny put it there. The whole crisis pivot comes from Roland's throwaway comment. Great economy and misdirection payoff.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, hello, I'm calling because I have a serious skin condition and I need to speak to someone immediately. It's very urgent. No, I don't have time to wait on hold. Yes, I understand you have other patients, but this is a dermatological emergency. What do you mean 'how bad is it'? Well, my complexion has taken on a rather sallow, almost gray undertone, and there's a slight dryness around my T-zone that's absolutely unacceptable. My moisturizer isn't absorbing properly and I can see the beginning of what might be a fine line—possibly two fine lines—near my left eye. It's catastrophic.

jokes.text: Well, I talked to a few florists, and then I... decided it was more authentic and... economical, if I just went out back and... picked some.
jokes.setup: They demand to know where the flowers in the motel came from
jokes.punchline: Johnny's sheepish trailing explanation — the pauses as he realizes mid-sentence what he's about to admit — that he 'went out back and picked some' wildflowers including, apparently, poison oak
jokes.explanation: The pauses in Johnny's delivery are the comedy — 'and... decided it was more authentic and... economical' tells us he's constructing the justification as he speaks. Good cringe escalation as the horrible implication becomes clear.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Dad, this isn't funny.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, I think it's hilarious.

jokes.text: Uh, correct me if I'm wrong here, Johnny, but I don't think poison oak is that expensive.
jokes.setup: Johnny justified picking wildflowers as 'economical'
jokes.punchline: Roland's observation that poison oak is not expensive regardless — skewering Johnny's 'economical' logic by pointing out you could probably get it for free anyway
jokes.explanation: Excellent line — Roland's observation is technically correct and completely eviscerates Johnny's justification. The comedy is in the deadpan logic: poison oak isn't cheap to buy because you can't buy it, making 'economical' a meaningless defense. High economy and earned quality.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I have something to tell you. We've lost everything.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: What? How is that possible?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, it turns out I'm not responsible for this one. Your mother is.

jokes.text: Wait a second, if you picked them, then how come you don't have the rash? / Well, I was wearing gardening gloves.
jokes.setup: Everyone has a rash except Johnny who supposedly handled the poison oak
jokes.punchline: The simple explanation — he wore gardening gloves — is both completely sensible and somehow the funniest possible answer in the context
jokes.explanation: Good beat — the setup builds mystery (why doesn't Johnny have it?), and the answer is so mundane and practical that it's funny. Johnny's obliviousness to the chaos he caused while being the only properly-equipped person is a nice irony.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh, I'm sorry, I didn't realize that a normal, healthy person isn't supposed to smell flowers. What was I thinking? Next you'll tell me I shouldn't be touching things or breathing air.

jokes.text: So... someone's gonna have to tell David before he... takes it all back. / No offense, Mr. Rose, but I'm pretty sure that someone is not going to be me.
jokes.setup: Johnny and Stevie realize David needs to be told his moisturizer isn't the problem — and therefore he doesn't need to cancel everything
jokes.punchline: Stevie's flat refusal — 'that someone is not going to be me' — delivered to Johnny's face with complete candor, leaving him to face David alone
jokes.explanation: Good Stevie-Johnny dynamic — her absolute refusal to be the one to tell David (who will be furious either way) is delivered with complete Stevie directness. The 'no offense, Mr. Rose' politeness before abandoning him is a nice touch.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I may have been slightly harsh with the vendor.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I called him and I said, 'Your flowers are garbage, your arrangement is an insult to floristry, and you should be ashamed of yourself.' Then I hung up.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It turns out the flowers were fine. I was just having a bad day.

jokes.text: Well, there was that summer that Jimmy Smits stole my heart on stage in a workshop-only production of 'An Officer and a Gentleman'. I suppose that might be called emotional embezzlement?
jokes.setup: The lawyer asks Moira if she has any personal experiences similar to the embezzlement case that might affect her judgment
jokes.punchline: Moira reframes a romantic experience with Jimmy Smits as 'emotional embezzlement' — a completely unrelated, theatrical personal anecdote delivered as a serious legal disclosure
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. 'Emotional embezzlement' is an inspired coinage — it sounds legal, it's romantically dramatic, and it completely derails the courtroom proceeding. The specificity of 'workshop-only production' signals Moira's theatrical world with precision. Only Moira would interpret a legal question about financial crime through a romantic theatrical lens and find genuine equivalence. Very high character integration and originality. Highly quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Ted, are you giving the puppies a pep talk?

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Well, I was, but I guess you could say it's more of a pup talk.

jokes.text: So your answer is no. / To what?
jokes.setup: The lawyer tries to redirect Moira's answer back to the actual legal question after her Jimmy Smits tangent
jokes.punchline: Moira's genuine 'To what?' — she has completely lost track of the original question, and possibly doesn't care
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy — two words ('To what?') do everything. It's the ideal punctuation to the Jimmy Smits speech. The lawyer's growing exasperation implied in 'So your answer is no' makes the 'To what?' land as either genuine confusion or magnificent defiance. High character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: My mind has just been elsewhere lately.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Well, your mind being elsewhere is kind of your default work style.

jokes.text: The fact that my own world was ripped out from under me, by someone like this prick of a prestidigitator?
jokes.setup: Moira is asked whether she has personal conflicts affecting her judgment of the embezzlement case
jokes.punchline: She dramatically reframes the Rose family losing their fortune as equivalent to this embezzlement case, calling the business manager a 'prestidigitator' (magician who makes things disappear) — technically accurate but wildly theatrical
jokes.explanation: 'Prestidigitator' is an inspired word choice — it technically means a sleight-of-hand artist, which applies to the embezzler, but is so theatrical and Moira-specific that it derails the legal proceeding with vocabulary alone. This is peak Moira language — simultaneously appropriate and completely wrong-register for a courtroom.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I just... I really like you, and I thought maybe you felt the same way, and I guess I was wrong, so...

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Alexis, I do care about you, but I don't think we should—

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: No, no, it's fine. Totally fine. Actually, you know what? Can we just get a puppy?

jokes.text: May I approach the bench, please? / No, you may not. The Court thanks and excuses Prospective Juror 75.
jokes.setup: Moira, having thoroughly derailed jury selection, tries to use a legal procedural request
jokes.punchline: The judge's flat 'No, you may not' — followed immediately by dismissal — is the perfect judicial comedown for someone who announced a 'master class in judicatory persuasion'
jokes.explanation: Great payoff to Moira's confident prediction. 'No, you may not' is the perfect short circuit. The formal 'The Court thanks and excuses Prospective Juror 75' — the bureaucratic language dismissing this hurricane of a person — is the ideal comedic contrast.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Can I just get the saddest puppy you have? Like, the one that looks like it's been through the most? Because honestly, at this point, I feel like we deserve each other.

jokes.text: Jocelyn, for the sake of that woman, you must push for the maximum sentence!
jokes.setup: Moira has just been dismissed from jury duty; Jocelyn is still on the panel
jokes.punchline: Moira immediately tries to influence Jocelyn's verdict — which would itself be jury tampering — after being dismissed for being biased
jokes.explanation: Excellent character joke — Moira is dismissed for being unable to be impartial, and immediately commits a second (worse) act of jury interference. The obliviousness is perfect. Very high character integration.

jokes.text: You two know each other? / Not really, we just got to talking on the way in. / I... I wouldn't say we were close. / All right, I'm dismissing both Juror 75 and Juror 23.
jokes.setup: The judge catches Moira whispering to Jocelyn about the verdict
jokes.punchline: Both Moira and Jocelyn scrambling to minimize their relationship — 'not really,' 'I wouldn't say we were close' — making themselves look more suspicious, leading to both being dismissed
jokes.explanation: Good escalation — Moira's intervention takes down Jocelyn too, which is both a consequence and a comedy beat. The two women's simultaneous distancing ('not really' / 'I wouldn't say we were close') when they clearly know each other is funny. Jocelyn's casualness ('just got to talking') vs Moira's formal distancing is a good contrast.

jokes.text: Mother (bleep). / This is the farthest I've ever gone!
jokes.setup: Jocelyn is dismissed from jury duty right when she thought she was going to serve
jokes.punchline: 'This is the farthest I've ever gone!' — Jocelyn's genuine devastation at being dismissed from jury duty (a civic obligation most people try to avoid) as if she'd almost won a competition
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. 'This is the farthest I've ever gone!' perfectly inverts the audience's expectation — jury duty is something people want to escape, but Jocelyn experiences it as a competitive achievement. Her genuine devastation at elimination makes it cringe-comedy gold. The bleep on 'Mother (bleep)' adds to the comedic shock. Very high character integration — only Jocelyn would be heartbroken at jury dismissal.

jokes.text: Okay, well I don't know what to tell you, Brenda, I'm rashy! Rashy! Yeah, the batch must be contaminated or something, it's like, some, some cream-borne virus!
jokes.setup: David is on the phone with his supplier, trying to explain why they need to pull the products
jokes.punchline: 'Cream-borne virus' — David's dramatic medical coinage for what is actually contact dermatitis — is the escalation. 'I'm rashy! Rashy!' as a self-description is the setup.
jokes.explanation: 'Cream-borne virus' is a great David coinage — escalating a skin reaction to pathogen-level language. 'I'm rashy! Rashy!' as repeated self-description has good energy. High character integration — David would absolutely weaponize dramatic medical vocabulary.

jokes.text: My skin is normally fresh, and dewy, and blemish-free.
jokes.setup: David is describing his rash situation to his vendor
jokes.punchline: His earnest self-description of his usual skin quality — 'fresh, and dewy, and blemish-free' — when he currently looks like the Phantom of the Opera
jokes.explanation: David's vanity assertion about his usual skin quality during an active skin crisis is perfectly in character. 'Fresh, and dewy, and blemish-free' sounds like he's reading his own Grindr profile. Good character integration.

jokes.text: Well, does the vendor have a sense of humor, because this is a good one!
jokes.setup: Johnny arrives just as David is on the phone with the vendor blaming the moisturizer
jokes.punchline: Johnny's breezy 'this is a good one!' — knowing the moisturizer isn't at fault — frames a crisis about to get worse as amusing
jokes.explanation: Johnny's nervous levity as he prepares to drop the poison oak bombshell is a good character beat. His attempt to soften the blow by framing it as a funny story is very Johnny.

jokes.text: Brenda, can I call you back? You know, it seems poison oak accidentally found its way into the floral arrangements at the motel. / How did that happen? / I don't know. I don't know how it happened. I think someone, someone must've thought it was a beautiful plant.
jokes.setup: David cuts off the vendor call and Johnny delivers the poison oak news as gently as possible
jokes.punchline: Johnny's completely transparent cover story — 'someone must've thought it was a beautiful plant' — spoken directly in front of David who can clearly see through him
jokes.explanation: Johnny's attempt to euphemize his own mistake in real-time is a good cringe beat. 'Someone must've thought it was a beautiful plant' is the kind of transparent deflection that's funny because both parties know exactly who 'someone' is.

jokes.text: Mm-hmm. You did this, didn't you? / Well... you, yourself must've thought it was a pretty flower, or you wouldn't have put your whole face in it!
jokes.setup: David has deduced that Johnny is responsible; Johnny tries to deflect
jokes.punchline: Johnny's defense — that David must've found the poison oak attractive since he put his whole face in it — is an absolutely desperate reversal that accidentally points out David's own culpability
jokes.explanation: Johnny deflecting by pointing out David buried his face in poison oak is a perfect comedic reversal — and it's technically true. 'Your whole face in it' is a devastating and funny detail. The logic is absurd (appreciating beauty doesn't excuse planting poison oak) but delivered with such desperation that it works brilliantly. Justifies 8+ as one of the episode's best exchanges.

jokes.text: Well, you, yourself must've thought it was a pretty flower, or you wouldn't have put your whole face in it! / I'm sorry for touching my nose to a flower!
jokes.setup: Johnny has confessed (sort of) to putting poison oak in the arrangements
jokes.punchline: David reveals that Moira got the rash because she literally put her face in the flowers — 'your whole face in it' — and Johnny's apologetic response 'I'm sorry for touching my nose to a flower!' is the punchline
jokes.explanation: 'I'm sorry for touching my nose to a flower!' is a great Johnny line — the specific and slightly formal phrasing ('touching my nose to a flower') makes it funnier than 'I'm sorry for smelling it.' The indignant specificity of the apology is the joke. High quotability.

jokes.text: I'm sorry for touching my nose to a flower!
jokes.setup: Johnny has accused David of essentially complicity by smelling the flower
jokes.punchline: David's furious, sarcastic 'apology' for the basic act of smelling a flower — framed as though this is what he's in trouble for — is a great ironic response
jokes.explanation: The precise specificity of 'touching my nose to a flower' is wonderful — David is essentially apologizing for the most innocent possible act while being furious about the outrageous thing that caused it. The sarcastic formality of the apology is perfect David.

jokes.text: I just went all Gordon Ramsay on one of my most important vendors! I basically told her, her product was like a jar of Ebola!
jokes.setup: David realizes he berated his vendor for a contamination problem that was actually caused by his own father's poison oak
jokes.punchline: 'A jar of Ebola' — David's hyperbolic description of what he accused the vendor's moisturizer of being
jokes.explanation: 'A jar of Ebola' is a great David hyperbole — combining the biological severity framing he was using (cream-borne virus) with the specific awfulness of what he said to the vendor. High character integration.

jokes.text: So, this is uh, not a good time to talk about future orders. / We will be canceling your account.
jokes.setup: Johnny tries to defuse the situation with the vendor
jokes.punchline: David's flat 'We will be canceling your account' — immediate, businesslike, and directed at the vendor he just verbally attacked — is a great reversal where HE is the one being fired
jokes.explanation: Excellent inversion — David attacked the vendor, and now the vendor fires him. 'We will be canceling your account' delivered as a flat announcement by David is funny for its authority-voice quality in a context where he has no standing. Good economy.

jokes.text: Hi. / Hi. / How long have you been standing there? / Um, long enough. / Yeah, I was just giving them a little pup talk.
jokes.setup: Alexis realizes Ted has been watching her give the dogs a prep talk
jokes.punchline: 'Pup talk' instead of 'pep talk' — a pun that's embarrassingly charming, and delivered with the confidence of someone who thinks this is quite clever
jokes.explanation: 'Pup talk' is a clean, effective pun — the kind that makes you groan and grin simultaneously. In context (she was literally talking to puppies), it's perfectly set up. Alexis delivering it with casual pride is the right tone. Good economy.

jokes.text: this is like a very cute look for me.
jokes.setup: Alexis is trying to tell Ted she's in love with him — a serious, vulnerable moment
jokes.punchline: Mid-confession, she pauses to note that her current professional vibe is 'a very cute look for me' — a completely Alexis self-assessment in the middle of an emotional declaration
jokes.explanation: A beautifully character-specific joke — Alexis can't help self-commenting on her own attractiveness even during a heartfelt confession. It's funny and also revealing: she uses humor and vanity as a shield before vulnerability. High character integration. The 'Okay. Not finished yet.' that follows is the perfect structural pause.

jokes.text: Oh, I just assumed that's how you always work.
jokes.setup: Alexis says she's been having a hard time focusing because her mind has been elsewhere
jokes.punchline: Ted's gentle, fond, completely honest observation that her distracted working style is not unusual — 'I just assumed that's how you always work' — is a perfect straight-man line
jokes.explanation: Very well-calibrated Ted line — affectionate, honest, and gently funny. It's not a mean joke about Alexis; it's a fond observation that's also completely accurate. The fact that she briefly accepts it ('Thank you, Ted') before redirecting is a nice comedic beat. Economy is excellent.

jokes.text: Um, but maybe I could take a puppy home for the night. / You know I can't let you do that. / Yeah, no. That's no problem.
jokes.setup: Alexis has just declared her love to Ted and said he doesn't have to say anything
jokes.punchline: She immediately pivots to asking to take a puppy home — the emotional pivot is both funny and revealing
jokes.explanation: Perfectly character-specific pivot — after a moment of genuine vulnerability, Alexis immediately reaches for something concrete and adorable (a puppy) as emotional cover. The speed of the pivot is the joke. Ted's gentle refusal and Alexis's immediate casual 'Yeah, no. That's no problem' is the comedic deflation. High character integration.

jokes.text: Um, I was just thinking if there was like, a particularly runty one, like Cindy, or like, no, no. That's okay.
jokes.setup: Ted has refused to let her take a puppy home; the conversation should be over
jokes.punchline: Alexis keeps negotiating — she has a specific runt in mind ('like Cindy') but walks herself back mid-sentence, knowing it's over
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'Cindy' is the key — Alexis has clearly already named the runt she wants and has been thinking about this, making the 'or like, no, no' walk-back funnier. It shows how deep her puppy feelings go even in the middle of a love confession aftermath.

jokes.text: Okay, well, this is going to be a very successful event. Like, really great.
jokes.setup: Alexis has just been rejected for taking a puppy, the love confession hangs in the air, and the scene needs to end
jokes.punchline: Alexis's pivot to professional optimism — 'this is going to be a very successful event. Like, really great.' — is simultaneously avoidant and desperately cheerful
jokes.explanation: Good character beat — Alexis using professional enthusiasm to exit an emotionally charged moment is very true to her character development. The 'Like, really great' trailing addition signals she's not quite as okay as she's performing.

tmdb_overview: Moira tries to avoid jury duty and David issues a recall on a Rose Apothecary product that causes rashes.
insight: Character escalations drive a solid 77, but sparse punchline moments limit its elite potential.
